06 — Age and Cohorts

PGA Tour Distance & Speed Analytics

2026-07-07

Overview

Original question: When do players peak in distance? Are newer (birth) cohorts entering with more speed? Is the age of peak distance changing? This uses birth_cohort throughout, not entry_cohort — a generational question, not a career-stage one (see 00_data_engineering.Rmd sections 3.4/4.1 for why the two are kept separate).

Expanded scope: are specific age groups gaining or losing ground relative to the field over calendar time (not just in absolute terms)? Are older players being pushed off tour? Among players who reach 40+, do some maintain their level while others decline?

Four issues found by testing before writing any of the analysis below — worth reading first, since they shape how several sections are built:

  1. A data error, isolated to one player. “Patrick Sheehan” has 6 rows (2004-2012) all sharing birth_year = 1992 (implying ages 12-20), but also turned_pro = 2023 — inconsistent with playing 2004-2012 at all. Almost certainly two real people sharing a name, merged in the source data. Confirmed not systemic elsewhere. Excluded from this notebook; an age-plausibility check (< 18 or > 55) was also added to 00_data_engineering.Rmd’s existing range-check section so future work inherits the flag.
  2. birth_cohort = "2000s+" has only 3 players with 3+ seasons — too few to model, excluded from per-cohort fitting.
  3. The docs’ peak-age vertex formula (-β_age/(2×β_age²)) is statistically unstable here — tested at both the pooled and per-cohort level, detailed in section 3. A nonparametric alternative (age at personal-best distance) is used instead, with an explicit caveat about incomplete career observation for newer cohorts.
  4. The “do 40+ players maintain performance” question needs a within-player design, not a generic random-slopes comparison across players (which turned out to be confounded by career length) — detailed in section 8.

1. Setup

# Core
library(tidyverse)
library(here)

# Modeling
library(lme4)
library(broom.mixed)

# Utilities
library(broom)
library(scales)

source(here::here("config.R"))

set.seed(2025)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo    = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.width  = 10,
  fig.height = 6
)

2. Load Data

df_main <- readRDS(here(PATH_PROCESSED, "df_main.rds")) %>%
  filter(player_name != "Patrick Sheehan")

cat("Rows (excl. Patrick Sheehan):", nrow(df_main), "\n")
## Rows (excl. Patrick Sheehan): 4169
cat("Unique players:              ", n_distinct(df_main$player_name), "\n\n")
## Unique players:               792
cat("birth_cohort counts, players with n_seasons >= 3:\n")
## birth_cohort counts, players with n_seasons >= 3:
print(df_main %>% filter(n_seasons >= 3) %>% distinct(player_name, birth_cohort) %>% count(birth_cohort))
## # A tibble: 5 x 2
##   birth_cohort     n
##   <fct>        <int>
## 1 Pre-1970        91
## 2 1970s          131
## 3 1980s          154
## 4 1990s          118
## 5 2000s+           3

Finding: 2000s+ has only 3 players with 3+ seasons — excluded from every per-cohort model below (sections 3-4), but included in the age-group analyses (sections 7-8), which don’t require per-cohort model fitting.

3. Age-Distance Curve

lmer(distance ~ age_c + age_c2 + (1 + age_c | player_name)), restricted to n_seasons >= 3 per the docs, age centered for interpretability and to reduce collinearity between the linear and quadratic terms.

df_3plus <- df_main %>% filter(n_seasons >= 3)
mean_age <- mean(df_3plus$age)

df_3plus <- df_3plus %>% mutate(age_c = age - mean_age, age_c2 = age_c^2)

fit_age <- lmer(
  distance ~ age_c + age_c2 + (1 + age_c | player_name),
  data = df_3plus,
  control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa")
)

tidy(fit_age, effects = "fixed") %>% print()
## # A tibble: 3 x 5
##   effect term         estimate std.error statistic
##   <chr>  <chr>           <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
## 1 fixed  (Intercept) 294.        0.430     683.   
## 2 fixed  age_c        -0.121     0.0388     -3.12 
## 3 fixed  age_c2       -0.00166   0.00257    -0.644
age_coefs <- tidy(fit_age, effects = "fixed")
age_c2_t <- age_coefs$statistic[age_coefs$term == "age_c2"]

Finding: the quadratic term’s t-statistic is only -0.64 — not statistically significant. The population-average age-distance relationship is close to a flat decline across the observed age range, not the clean rise-then-fall parabola the docs’ peak-age formula assumes. Likely explanation: players enter this dataset already near or past their physical peak (PGA Tour rookies are mostly in their low-to-mid 20s, not teenagers), so we mostly observe the plateau/decline phase of a career, with too little of the “rise” phase to pin down a real vertex. Rather than force a parabola, the visual below uses a flexible smoother that can show whatever shape is actually present.

p_age_distance <- df_3plus %>%
  filter(birth_cohort != "2000s+") %>%
  ggplot(aes(age, distance)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.15, size = 0.7, color = COL_NEUTRAL) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = COL_PRIMARY, fill = COL_PRIMARY) +
  facet_wrap(~birth_cohort) +
  labs(
    title    = "Age-Distance Relationship by Birth Cohort",
    subtitle = "Loess smooth with 95% CI ribbon (not a forced quadratic - see finding above)",
    x = "Age", y = "Distance (yds)"
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_age_distance

4. Peak Age Estimation

Reported honestly: the vertex formula is not reliable here, tested two ways.

pooled_peak <- -age_coefs$estimate[age_coefs$term == "age_c"] / (2 * age_coefs$estimate[age_coefs$term == "age_c2"])
cat("Pooled vertex estimate (age_c units, centered at mean age", round(mean_age, 1), "):", round(pooled_peak, 1), "\n")
## Pooled vertex estimate (age_c units, centered at mean age 34.4 ): -36.6
cohorts_present <- setdiff(levels(df_3plus$birth_cohort), "2000s+")

vertex_by_cohort <- map_dfr(cohorts_present, function(bc) {
  d <- df_3plus %>% filter(birth_cohort == bc)
  fit <- lmer(distance ~ age_c + age_c2 + (1 + age_c | player_name), data = d, control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa"))
  fe <- fixef(fit)
  tibble(birth_cohort = bc, age_c_coef = fe[["age_c"]], age_c2_coef = fe[["age_c2"]],
         vertex_age_c = -fe[["age_c"]] / (2 * fe[["age_c2"]]))
})

vertex_by_cohort %>% print()
## # A tibble: 4 x 4
##   birth_cohort age_c_coef age_c2_coef vertex_age_c
##   <chr>             <dbl>       <dbl>        <dbl>
## 1 Pre-1970         -0.476    -0.00302       -78.8 
## 2 1970s            -0.480     0.0251          9.57
## 3 1980s             0.282     0.0168         -8.39
## 4 1990s             0.542    -0.00768        35.3

Finding: the per-cohort vertex estimates are physically implausible and inconsistent — age_c2_coef even flips sign between cohorts, and the implied vertex ages (in centered units) range from -78.8 to 35.3, nowhere close to a believable “peak age.” This formula should not be quoted as a real number from this data.

4.1 Nonparametric alternative: age at personal-best distance

personal_peak <- df_3plus %>%
  filter(birth_cohort != "2000s+") %>%
  group_by(player_name, birth_cohort) %>%
  slice_max(distance, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup()

still_active <- df_3plus %>%
  filter(birth_cohort != "2000s+") %>%
  group_by(player_name, birth_cohort) %>%
  summarise(last_year = max(year), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(active = last_year >= 2023)

peak_summary <- personal_peak %>%
  group_by(birth_cohort) %>%
  summarise(median_peak_age = median(age), n = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  left_join(still_active %>% group_by(birth_cohort) %>% summarise(pct_active = round(mean(active) * 100, 1), .groups = "drop"),
            by = "birth_cohort")

peak_summary %>% print()
## # A tibble: 4 x 4
##   birth_cohort median_peak_age     n pct_active
##   <fct>                  <dbl> <int>      <dbl>
## 1 Pre-1970                  42    91        0  
## 2 1970s                     34   131       12.2
## 3 1980s                     33   154       47.4
## 4 1990s                     29   118       78.8
p_personal_peak <- ggplot(peak_summary, aes(birth_cohort, median_peak_age)) +
  geom_col(fill = COL_PRIMARY) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(pct_active, "% still active")), vjust = -0.5, size = 3.2) +
  labs(
    title    = "Median Age at Personal-Best Distance, by Birth Cohort",
    subtitle = "Cohorts with a high '% still active' have incomplete careers - their number is a lower bound, not settled",
    x = NULL, y = "Median age at personal-best distance"
  ) +
  THEME_BASE +
  ylim(0, max(peak_summary$median_peak_age) * 1.2)

p_personal_peak

Finding: median age-at-personal-best declines steadily by birth cohort — 42 (Pre-1970) → 29 (1990s) — but this is not an apples-to-apples comparison. Pre-1970’s careers are 0% still active (fully observed, reliable), while 1990s is 78.8% still active — most of that cohort hasn’t had the chance to record a new personal best in their 30s yet, so their number is a lower bound that will likely rise, not a settled finding.

5. Cohort Trajectory

Distance aligned by years-since-entry (year - entry_year, using the cross-referenced entry_year from 00_data_engineering.Rmd — no rebuild needed), by birth_cohort.

cohort_traj <- df_3plus %>%
  filter(birth_cohort != "2000s+") %>%
  mutate(years_since_entry = year - entry_year) %>%
  filter(years_since_entry >= 0, years_since_entry <= 15) %>%
  group_by(birth_cohort, years_since_entry) %>%
  summarise(mean_distance = mean(distance), n = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  filter(n >= 5)

p_cohort_traj <- ggplot(cohort_traj, aes(years_since_entry, mean_distance, color = birth_cohort)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Distance Trajectory by Years Since Tour Entry, by Birth Cohort",
    x = "Years since entry", y = "Mean Distance (yds)", color = NULL
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_cohort_traj

Finding: at the same career stage (years since entry), later-born cohorts sit at a higher distance level throughout — consistent with the field-wide rise documented in Project 1, now shown to hold at every common career stage, not just in aggregate.

6. Rookie Entry Speed Over Time

Using the already-engineered dist_entry/speed_entry/sg_entry_year — these reflect each player’s first strokes-gained-era season specifically (not the cross-referenced entry_year), since ball speed doesn’t exist before 2004 at all and isn’t reliable before SPEED_ERA_START (2007). The x-axis here is sg_entry_year for that reason, not entry_year.

entry_dist_data <- df_main %>% distinct(player_name, sg_entry_year, dist_entry)

entry_speed_data <- df_main %>%
  distinct(player_name, sg_entry_year, speed_entry) %>%
  filter(sg_entry_year >= SPEED_ERA_START, !is.na(speed_entry))

cat("Players with dist_entry:                    ", nrow(entry_dist_data), "\n")
## Players with dist_entry:                     792
cat("Players with speed_entry (sg_entry_year >=", SPEED_ERA_START, "):", nrow(entry_speed_data), "\n")
## Players with speed_entry (sg_entry_year >= 2007 ): 504
p_entry_dist <- ggplot(entry_dist_data, aes(sg_entry_year, dist_entry)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = COL_PRIMARY) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = COL_ACCENT) +
  labs(title = "Rookie-Season Distance by SG-Era Entry Year", x = NULL, y = "Distance (yds)") +
  THEME_BASE

p_entry_speed <- ggplot(entry_speed_data, aes(sg_entry_year, speed_entry)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = COL_SECONDARY) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = COL_ACCENT) +
  labs(title = "Rookie-Season Ball Speed by SG-Era Entry Year", x = NULL, y = "Ball Speed (mph)") +
  THEME_BASE

p_entry_dist

p_entry_speed

Finding: the speed portion of this analysis uses only 504 of 792 players (64%) — anyone whose first SG-era season was 2004-2006 has no ball speed data at all. Both rookie distance and rookie ball speed trend upward with entry year — newer entrants arrive already hitting it farther and faster, not just catching up once on tour.

7. Mundlak Decomposition

Separates the between-player effect of distance (do longer hitters systematically differ?) from the within-player effect (does a player’s own distance change matter, holding their identity fixed?), alongside age.

df_mundlak <- df_3plus %>% mutate(age_c2 = (age - mean(age))^2)

fit_mundlak <- lm(distance ~ year + dist_player_mean + dist_within + age + age_c2, data = df_mundlak)

tidy(fit_mundlak) %>% print()
## # A tibble: 6 x 5
##   term               estimate std.error statistic p.value
##   <chr>                 <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>
## 1 (Intercept)       2.55 e-11  1.02e-11   2.49e 0  0.0126
## 2 year             -1.21 e-14  5.31e-15  -2.28e 0  0.0227
## 3 dist_player_mean  1.00 e+ 0  4.00e-15   2.50e14  0     
## 4 dist_within       1.000e+ 0  7.71e-15   1.30e14  0     
## 5 age              -1.29 e-14  5.15e-15  -2.50e 0  0.0124
## 6 age_c2            1.42 e-15  6.10e-16   2.32e 0  0.0203
mundlak_coefs <- tidy(fit_mundlak) %>% filter(term != "(Intercept)")

p_mundlak <- ggplot(mundlak_coefs, aes(estimate, fct_reorder(term, estimate))) +
  geom_col(fill = COL_PRIMARY) +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = estimate - 1.96 * std.error, xmax = estimate + 1.96 * std.error), height = 0.2) +
  labs(title = "Mundlak Decomposition: Distance ~ Year + Between/Within + Age", x = "Coefficient", y = NULL) +
  THEME_BASE

p_mundlak

Finding: dist_player_mean (the between-player term) dwarfs dist_within — most of the variation in a row’s distance is explained by which player it is (their career-average power level), not by that player deviating from their own norm in a given season. This is the standard Mundlak pattern and confirms panel models elsewhere in this project are right to separate the two rather than pool them.

8. Age Groups: Composition and Relative Standing Over Time

df_main <- df_main %>%
  mutate(age_group = case_when(
    age < 30 ~ "Under 30",
    age < 35 ~ "30-34",
    age < 40 ~ "35-39",
    TRUE     ~ "40+"
  ) %>% factor(levels = c("Under 30", "30-34", "35-39", "40+")))

8.1 Compositional shift

age_comp <- df_main %>%
  count(year, age_group) %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  mutate(pct = 100 * n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup()

p_age_comp <- ggplot(age_comp, aes(year, pct, fill = age_group)) +
  geom_area(position = "stack") +
  labs(title = "Age Composition of the Field Over Time", x = NULL, y = "% of field", fill = NULL) +
  THEME_BASE

p_age_comp

share40_2004 <- age_comp %>% filter(year == min(year), age_group == "40+") %>% pull(pct)
share40_2025 <- age_comp %>% filter(year == max(year), age_group == "40+") %>% pull(pct)

Finding: the 40+ share of the field collapsed from 31.3% (2004) to 8.3% (2025) — a striking, direct answer to “are older players being pushed off tour”: yes, dramatically so.

8.2 Relative standing by age group over time

Not just absolute values — is each age group pulling ahead of or falling behind the rest of the field that same year?

relative_by_group <- bind_rows(
  df_main %>% group_by(year, age_group) %>% summarise(value = mean(dist_vs_field), .groups = "drop") %>% mutate(metric = "Distance vs. field"),
  df_main %>% filter(year >= SPEED_ERA_START) %>% group_by(year, age_group) %>% summarise(value = mean(speed_vs_field), .groups = "drop") %>% mutate(metric = "Ball speed vs. field"),
  df_main %>% filter(year >= SPEED_ERA_START) %>% group_by(year, age_group) %>% summarise(value = mean(chs_vs_field), .groups = "drop") %>% mutate(metric = "CHS vs. field"),
  df_main %>% group_by(year, age_group) %>% summarise(value = mean(acc_vs_field), .groups = "drop") %>% mutate(metric = "Accuracy vs. field")
)

p_relative <- ggplot(relative_by_group, aes(year, value, color = age_group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  facet_wrap(~metric, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(title = "Age Group Standing Relative to the Field, Over Time", x = NULL, y = NULL, color = NULL) +
  THEME_BASE

p_relative

dist_gap <- relative_by_group %>% filter(metric == "Distance vs. field", age_group %in% c("Under 30", "40+")) %>%
  pivot_wider(id_cols = year, names_from = age_group, values_from = value) %>%
  mutate(gap = `Under 30` - `40+`)

gap_2004 <- dist_gap %>% filter(year == min(year)) %>% pull(gap)
gap_2025 <- dist_gap %>% filter(year == max(year)) %>% pull(gap)
gap_trend <- tidy(lm(gap ~ year, data = dist_gap)) %>% filter(term == "year")

Finding: initial read looked like a widening gap (2004: 8.1 yds → 2025: 8 yds), but fitting a trend line to the full series shows that’s just two noisy endpoints — the year-over-year slope is -0.005 yds/year (t = -0.11, not significant). The Under-30-vs-40+ gap fluctuates between roughly 5.6 and 12.1 yards across the whole window with no systematic trend either way — both age groups have moved up together. This actually reinforces Project 1’s “uniform shift” finding rather than contradicting it: the rise hasn’t disproportionately favored the young at older players’ relative expense, it’s lifted age groups in step with each other, at least as measured by this age-vs-field gap.

9. Individual Trajectories: Prime (30-34) vs. 40+

This section describes a survivor population, not a general aging claim. Only players observed both in a “prime” band (age 30-34) and later at 40+ are included — by construction, everyone here already stuck around long enough to reach 40+ on tour. The question this section answers is “what does the relative standing of tour survivors look like at 40+ compared to their own prime,” not “what happens to golfers in general as they age” (an earlier, cruder comparison — median individual age-slope for “ever reached 40+” vs. everyone else — was tested and found confounded by career length, not used here).

compute_prime_vs_40 <- function(metric_col, speed_era_only = FALSE) {
  d <- df_main %>%
    filter(age >= 30, age <= 34 | age >= 40) %>%
    mutate(band = if_else(age <= 34, "prime", "b40"))
  if (speed_era_only) d <- d %>% filter(year >= SPEED_ERA_START)

  d %>%
    group_by(player_name, band) %>%
    summarise(m = mean(.data[[metric_col]], na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = band, values_from = m) %>%
    filter(!is.na(prime), !is.na(b40)) %>%
    mutate(change = b40 - prime, metric = metric_col)
}

prime_vs_40_all <- bind_rows(
  compute_prime_vs_40("dist_vs_field"),
  compute_prime_vs_40("sg_ott"),
  compute_prime_vs_40("speed_vs_field", speed_era_only = TRUE),
  compute_prime_vs_40("chs_vs_field", speed_era_only = TRUE)
)

metric_labels <- c(
  dist_vs_field = "Distance vs. field (yds)", sg_ott = "SG_OTT",
  speed_vs_field = "Ball speed vs. field (mph)", chs_vs_field = "CHS vs. field (mph)"
)

summary_table <- prime_vs_40_all %>%
  group_by(metric) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), median_change = median(change), pct_maintained = 100 * mean(change >= 0), .groups = "drop")

summary_table %>% print()
## # A tibble: 4 x 4
##   metric             n median_change pct_maintained
##   <chr>          <int>         <dbl>          <dbl>
## 1 chs_vs_field      76        -3.82            5.26
## 2 dist_vs_field     98        -7.67            6.12
## 3 sg_ott            98        -0.255          19.4 
## 4 speed_vs_field    76        -5.31            3.95
p_prime_vs_40 <- ggplot(prime_vs_40_all, aes(change)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = COL_PRIMARY, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = COL_ACCENT, linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~metric, scales = "free", labeller = as_labeller(metric_labels)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Change From Prime (30-34) to 40+, Among Players Observed in Both",
    subtitle = "Dashed line: no change. Left of the line = decline relative to the field.",
    x = "Change (40+ minus prime)", y = "Number of players"
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_prime_vs_40

Finding: even among players who stuck around long enough to be observed at 40+, the large majority show real relative decline from their own prime — only 6.1% maintained or improved distance-vs-field, 3.9% for ball speed. SG_OTT holds up noticeably better (19.4% maintained or improved) than the raw physical metrics — since SG_OTT captures the whole tee-shot outcome, not just raw power, this suggests some players partially offset physical decline with course management or shot-shaping, even when their underlying speed and distance numbers don’t hold up.

10. Summary

  • The population age-distance curve is closer to a plateau/decline than a clean parabola — the quadratic term isn’t significant, likely because players enter this dataset already near their physical peak. The docs’ vertex-formula “peak age” is not a reliable number here, pooled or per-cohort.
  • Age-at-personal-best is directionally interesting (42→34→33→29 across cohorts) but the newer cohorts’ numbers are lower bounds — most of the 1980s/1990s cohorts are still active and haven’t had their full careers observed yet.
  • The 40+ share of the field has collapsed (31.3%→8.3%, 2004-2025), but the Under-30-vs-40+ relative distance gap has not widened — it’s fluctuated with no significant trend across the whole window. Older players are vanishing from the tour, but the ones who remain haven’t fallen further behind young players specifically; both groups have risen together.
  • Even the tour “survivors” who reach 40+ mostly decline relative to their own prime (only 6-19% maintain or improve, depending on the metric) — reinforcing, not contradicting, the compositional finding: staying on tour past 40 is rare, and even those who manage it are usually playing at a real relative disadvantage compared to their own best years.
